Subject: The pandemic masked Canadians' debt problem — what now?
Body: First Up Plus, international students facing deportation and cash for turning down your A/C ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ First Up By Manuela Vega Good morning. Here’s the latest on Canadians’ debt problems, international students facing deportation and Ontario’s new energy conservation initiative. DON’T MISS Canadian Press/Andrew Vaughan economy Canadians are hurting as their debt problems mount — and now the banks are suffering too At the start of the pandemic, interest rates were low, financial support was flowing and banks weren’t aggressively collecting debts. “It was really masking the fact that people were using credit to make ends meet,” one expert said. But now, collections and interest rates are up, he said, and the situation no longer seems manageable. A series of reports this week put it in black and white: Canada has the highest debt-to-GDP ratio in the G7, and credit and car loan delinquencies are increasing. Now, Canada’s Big Five banks have taken a hit, too, as they reported putting aside billions of dollars to protect themselves from bad loans. Christine Dobby reports on the “perfect storm” of debt problems. The aftermath: If banks are earning less, it could eventually become more difficult for Canadians to borrow money, an expert said. Go deeper: An April poll of about 1,500 Canadians found more than half were cutting back on non-essentials to cover their debt, while 43 per cent reported sacrificing on some essentials, and 26 per cent said they would consider taking on extra work. More: With delinquency rates for credit cards and car loans increasing, mortgage defaults could be next, a report warns. Canadian Press File Photo immigration This student can’t blame a consultant for submitting a bogus college admission letter, a tribunal hears Amritraj Singh Batth, 25, is one of several international students from India fighting to stay in Canada after applying for a student visa through the same agent. The uphill battle against deportation comes after their admission letters for post-secondary institutions were found to be bogus. Advocates have said that it’s the students who are victims, finding out only years after the fact that there was an issue with their documentation. But a Canada Border Services Agency told a tribunal Thursday that Batth could not be absolved of his responsibility by blaming an unscrupulous consultant. Nicholas Keung reports on the case and what awaits Batth if the finding is upheld. Context: Some of the international students in the investigation have been in Canada since 2017, completed their studies, received work permits and been on the path to permanent residence. Word from Batth: “If I would have gotten to know prior to coming to Canada, I would never have entered Canada … I never intended to come to Canada illegally. I wanted to have proper legal documentation to come here,” he told the tribunal under cross examination. Dreamstime Photo climate crisis Ontario will give you cash to turn down your A/C Do you have a heat pump or a smart thermostat and central air conditioning? The Ontario government would like to turn down your A/C by a few degrees when the electrical grid is straining under peak demand — and they’ll give you $75 for the right to do it for you, Marco Chown Oved reports. Here’s why Energy Minister Todd Smith says the new program is “a win for customers, a win for the climate and a win for the province.” More: “To be clear: participants are always in control and can opt-out by adjusting their thermostat without losing incentives,” Smith said. Word from the Green Party: The program is “a feeble attempt to slap a bandaid on a crumbling energy system that has worsened thanks to this government’s total mismanagement,” party leader Mike Schreiner said in a statement, adding that “other provinces and countries are moving full-speed ahead with investments in renewable energy.” Wait, what? Shortly after being elected in 2018, Doug Ford’s government cancelled more than 700 renewable energy projects and numerous energy conservation programs after years of industry fleeing the province and electricity demand stagnating. WHAT ELSE David Johnston forgot something important in his report — and it could give the reassurance Canadians need. Do 102 names fit on a ballot?! Here’s everything you need to know about voting in the mayoral election. Mélanie Joly’s office is upset over Conservative MP Leslyn Lewis and controversial preacher Charles McVety’s plan to host an Israeli minister. Mayoral candidates sparred over economic issues during the fourth debate in just two days. Here’s what you need to know. The trip was years in the making. This is what Canada’s Black politicians learned when they visited their U.S. peers. Toronto Pride is mulling cuts to the festival after policing and insurance costs more than tripled. Brampton is the most expensive Ontario city for car insurance. Here’s why the average premium is now over $2,700. The “mom types” trend is silly but irresistible. Which type are you: silky, crunchy, almond or … scrunchie? Did you give your child an old phone to play with? Officials say many of these devices are to blame for accidental 911 calls. Changing a personal brand can be a hard sell, as Elizabeth Holmes’s attempted makeover as a wholesome suburban mom shows. Freedom Mobile has unveiled its first nationwide plan since the Rogers-Shaw deal. From the best Cannes Film Festival in years, the Star’s critic picks the top 10 movies. POV Steve Russell/The Star Three mayoral debates in one day, six long hours and one surprising winner. CLOSE-UP Steve Russell/The Star NATHAN PHILLIPS SQUARE: Six-year-old Canadian Gabriel Duclayna smiles after his parents Kenneth Duclayna and Regine Obsequo are sworn in as Canadian Citizens on Thursday. They are among thirty-seven people from 11 different countries sworn in as new Canadian citizens on Toronto Newcomer Day, an annual event held in May. Thank you for reading. You can reach me and the First Up team at firstup@thestar.ca. Ashley will see you back here tomorrow. Or, if you'd prefer, you can unsubscribe from this newsletter by clicking the first link below. Subject: [zzzzteana] Playboy wants to go out with a bang
Body: The Scotsman - 22 August 2002 Playboy wants to go out with a bang AN AGEING Berlin playboy has come up with an unusual offer to lure women into his bed - by promising the last woman he sleeps with an inheritance of 250,000 (160,000). Rolf Eden, 72, a Berlin disco owner famous for his countless sex partners, said he could imagine no better way to die than in the arms of an attractive young woman - preferably under 30. "I put it all in my last will and testament - the last woman who sleeps with me gets all the money," Mr Eden told Bild newspaper. "I want to pass away in the most beautiful moment of my life. First a lot of fun with a beautiful woman, then wild sex, a final orgasm - and it will all end with a heart attack and then Im gone." Mr Eden, who is selling his nightclub this year, said applications should be sent in quickly because of his age. "It could end very soon," he said. ------------------------ Yahoo! Subject: Re: Branching
Body: On Tue, 2008-08-05 at 12:54 -0700, chromatic wrote: > On Tuesday 05 August 2008 12:35:50 Geoffrey Broadwell wrote: > > bugfixes that should be backported to one or more already released > > versions and re-released immediately. > > I can see patching the previous release in case of a critical bugfix, but if > we get in the habit of encouraging users to expect updates of anything older > than the previous stable release for free, we've doomed the project. That's why I was careful to say 'one or more'. As in greater than zero, but other than that it's a separate policy decision that I was not trying to address in my previous message. > Point releases every month. Major releases every three months. Agree, except I'd like to hear more about how you define a 'major release'. > Complete and > utter refusal to support users who expect that they can install Parrot 1.0 > and get free support from the mailing list or IRC for the next eight to ten > years. Half agree. I agree that we should only *directly* support a release for a limited time, though I think the minimum sane time would be "major release before current one" -- 3-6 months at any given moment, given your above schedule. In other words, just because we do a new 3 month release, doesn't mean we immediately de-support the one we did just 3 months ago. Now, I might argue for a longer direct support schedule than just 'most recent + 1', but I think any less than that can't work in real life. Beyond that, I think we need to explicitly acknowledge that distro packagers have a longer schedule to care about. While we may not support them directly, we still need to have a process in place to make sure they are notified about critical problems that may apply to previous releases, so that they can go back and check/patch their versions. We should also facilitate any process that will help different distros to help each other to backport our trunk fixes in a timely fashion. In short, we don't have to do the hard work for the distros ourselves, but we can't leave them out in the cold, either. -'f

Subject: [opensuse] installation via IPv6
Body: Hi all, Could it be that during installation IPv6 is not enabled? At work aswell as at home i've my own installation-server / gwdg-mirror. Identical setup, but at home i use for all my vhosts an V6 address. After a system is installed, i can upgrade or add aditional repo's without any problem with my IPv6 servers. However during the initial installation the installation source coming froman IPv6 address are not recognised. If i perform a DVD-installation, just before the first boot, i saw that the module "ipv6" is not loaded. I presume this could be the reason. Any suggestions how i can load this module in grub before installation takes place, so that i can add my own installationsources? Hans
text:
[opensuse] installation via IPv6

Hi all, Could it be that during installation IPv6 is not enabled? At work aswell as at home i've my own installation-server / gwdg-mirror. Identical setup, but at home i use for all my vhosts an V6 address. After a system is installed, i can upgrade or add aditional repo's without any problem with my IPv6 servers. However during the initial installation the installation source coming froman IPv6 address are not recognised. If i perform a DVD-installation, just before the first boot, i saw that the module "ipv6" is not loaded. I presume this could be the reason. Any suggestions how i can load this module in grub before installation takes place, so that i can add my own installationsources? Hans

Subject: 21 lives lost in Uvalde one year ago
Body: Investigation of police response continues. Advertisement eNewspaper | Crosswords | Horoscopes Read in browser Read in browser The Daily Briefing YOUR MORNING NEWS ROUNDUP Wed May 24 2023 Nicole Fallert | Newsletter Writer 21 lives lost in Uvalde one year ago Investigation of police response continues. Advertisement Wednesday marks one year since the deadliest school shooting in Texas history. And multiple updates from Florida: Gov. Ron DeSantis will announce his 2024 bid for president in a Twitter Space with Tesla CEO Elon Musk and a Miami-Dade County school has banned elementary students from reading "The Hill We Climb" poem Amanda Gorman delivered at the 2021 presidential inauguration. 🙋🏼‍♀️ I'm Nicole Fallert, Daily Briefing author. The surgeon general has voiced concerned about social media's impact on young people. Here are some tips for safer scrolling. Now, here we go with Wednesday's news. One year later, Uvalde victims' families are still fighting for change Schools in Uvalde, Texas, will be closed Wednesday to commemorate one year since the heartbreaking massacre at Robb Elementary School, the deadliest school shooting in Texas history. The massacre left a lasting impact on the small town and devastated the families of the 21 victims, who are still grieving and fighting for accountability and gun reform. •The continuing investigation underlines the lasting fallout over the shooting and how the days after the attack were marred by authorities giving inaccurate and conflicting accounts.•Despite the nation’s growing epidemic of gun violence and mass shootings, Congress has been slow to act since Uvalde – only passing the Bipartisan Safer Communities Act, the largest gun reform bill in three decades.• But the legislation does not come close to the changes Uvalde families want to see — including a federal ban on assault weapons, red flag laws and requiring background checks on all gun purchases. A memorial stands in Uvalde, Texas, where where 19 students and two teachers were killed in a mass shooting at Robb Elementary School. Texas State University multimedia journalism staff Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis will kick off 2024 presidential bid SpaceX founder and Twitter owner Elon Musk will help launch Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis' presidential campaign into orbit Wednesday with an event hosted on the social media site. Musk boasts a massive Twitter audience and is one of the richest people in the world. While he has said he will not be formally endorsing DeSantis during the event, his financial backing could be pivotal for DeSantis if he chooses to do so. Former President Donald Trump's campaign mocked DeSantis' planned Twitter announcement, calling it "one of the most out-of-touch campaign launches in modern history." Read more •Ron DeSantis, like Donald Trump, says he can create a more conservative Supreme Court. •Musk's Twitter tries to recapture the political spotlight post-Trump with DeSantis 2024 run.•She stormed the Capitol on Jan. 6. Then Gov. Ron DeSantis made her a state regulator. Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis signs legislation on Monday, May 15, 2023, banning state funding for diversity, equity, and inclusion programs at Florida's public universities, at New College of Florida in Sarasota, Fla. Douglas R. Clifford, Tampa Bay Times via AP More news to know now •What we know about a deadly Border Patrol shooting of an Arizona tribal member. •Could the 14th Amendment solve the debt limit standoff? The White House says it's not a fix.•Russia extended the detainment of American reporter Evan Gershkovich by three months. •The increasing imbalance of global wildlife signals a looming ''sixth extinction.''•On today's 5 Things podcast, Austin American-Statesman State Politics Reporter Niki Griswold reflects on the Uvalde shooting, one year later. Listen on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or your smart speaker. What's the weather today? Check your local forecast here. South Carolina Senate passes 6-week abortion ban A new abortion access battle is poised to begin all over again as a bill which bans abortions after six weeks of conception with exceptions for rape and incest is headed to South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster's desk to become law. S.474 was passed Tuesday by the state's male dominated, Republican-controlled Senate. Five female senators — three Republicans, a Democrat and an Independent — continued efforts to filibuster the ban. Now abortion advocates have promised litigation as soon as the law takes effect. Read more •''A toxic issue'': How voters are forcing GOP contenders to talk about abortion ahead of 2024. •The Texas Senate advanced Medicaid expansion for new moms but added an abortion restriction.•HIV rates among young people are declining. But disparities persist. Florida school bans elementary students from reading Amanda Gorman's 'The Hill We Climb' poem Officials at a school in Miami-Dade County, Florida, on Tuesday banned elementary students from reading "The Hill We Climb," the poem Amanda Gorman famously recited at President Joe Biden's 2021 presidential inauguration. The poem, which has been published as a short book, will now be accessible only to middle school students at the Bob Graham Education Center in Miami Lakes. The former and first-ever National Youth Poet Laureate wrote on Instagram that she was "gutted" and said her publisher – Penguin Random House – is joining PEN America and others in a lawsuit to challenge book restrictions. Read more •The nation's largest LGBTQ advocacy group joined others in issuing a Florida travel advisory. •Florida restaurant Hamburger Mary's sues DeSantis for law restricting drag performances.•Target removed LGBTQ Pride month merchandise after threats. Youth Poet Laureate Amanda Gorman speaks during the inauguration of U.S. President Joe Biden on the West Front of the U.S. Capitol on January 20, 2021 in Washington, DC. Alex Wong, Getty Images Just for subscribers: •Mississippi River lock-and-dam system is outdated and in disrepair. What if it fails? •''Brighton ax murder'' case: James Krauseneck appeals conviction from beyond the grave. •Kratom supplier owes millions to family of South Florida nurse who overdosed and died. •''The show must go on'': Students raise $80K to perform canceled play with LGBTQ+ characters. These articles are for USA TODAY subscribers. You can sign up here. Brazilians protest outside Spanish consulate after Vinicius Júnior's racism row About 100 Brazilian protesters gathered outside the Spanish consulate in São Paulo on Tuesday to condemn the racist abuse of Real Madrid forward Vinícius Júnior in Spain’s top-flight soccer league. The demonstrations come as three people have been detained in Spain in connection with racist taunts aimed at the Brazilian soccer star. The incident occurred Sunday at Valencia, when Vinícius, playing for Real Madrid, was met with racist jeers before getting off the team bus and was the subject of racially tinged chants during the match. Read more •Barcelona loses to Valladolid, Raphinha shows message of support for Vinícius Júnior. A protester holds up a photo of Brazilian soccer star Vinicius Jr and a message that reads in Portuguese; "Fight racism" during a protest outside the Spanish Consulate in Sao Paulo, Brazil, Tuesday, May 23, 2023. Tuane Fernandes, AP Quick hits •MIT students gave a legendary linear algebra professor a standing ovation in their last lecture. •When should you have sex with someone new? Consider "box theory" before deciding.•Jayson Tatum scored 33 points, as the Celtics topped the Heat in Game 4.• Here are eight businesses run by AAPI women you should know about.•Netflix launched paid account sharing in the United States. Here's how much it will cost you. Photo of the day: Niall Horan claims 'The Voice' victory with Gina Miles win The final showdown for the Season 23 title was between Team Blake singer Grace West and Team Niall singer Gina Miles Tuesday night. West performed a soaring cover of Shelton’s “Lonely Tonight” on the finale stage, and she was joined by her loyal coach for the performance. Miles and Horan slowed things down with a soulful rendition of “New York State of Mind” by Billy Joel. It turns out Horan made the right choice in the end, as Miles was declared the winner of Season 23, giving the freshman coach his first victory on the show. Read more Gina Miles and Niall Horan sing “New York State of Mind” Tuesday night. Trae Patton, NBC Nicole Fallert is a newsletter writer at USA TODAY, sign up for the email here. Want to send Nicole a note? Shoot her an email at NFallert@usatoday.com or follow along with her musings on Twitter. Support journalism like this – subscribe to USA TODAY here. The massacre left a lasting impact on the small town and devastated the families of the 21 victims, who are still grieving and fighting for accountability and gun reform. •The continuing investigation underlines the lasting fallout over the shooting and how the days after the attack were marred by authorities giving inaccurate and conflicting accounts.•Despite the nation’s growing epidemic of gun violence and mass shootings, Congress has been slow to act since Uvalde – only passing the Bipartisan Safer Communities Act, the largest gun reform bill in three decades.• But the legislation does not come close to the changes Uvalde families want to see — including a federal ban on assault weapons, red flag laws and requiring background checks on all gun purchases. A memorial stands in Uvalde, Texas, where where 19 students and two teachers were killed in a mass shooting at Robb Elementary School. Texas State University multimedia journalism staff Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis will kick off 2024 presidential bid SpaceX founder and Twitter owner Elon Musk will help launch Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis' presidential campaign into orbit Wednesday with an event hosted on the social media site. Musk boasts a massive Twitter audience and is one of the richest people in the world. While he has said he will not be formally endorsing DeSantis during the event, his financial backing could be pivotal for DeSantis if he chooses to do so. Former President Donald Trump's campaign mocked DeSantis' planned Twitter announcement, calling it "one of the most out-of-touch campaign launches in modern history." Read more •Ron DeSantis, like Donald Trump, says he can create a more conservative Supreme Court. •Musk's Twitter tries to recapture the political spotlight post-Trump with DeSantis 2024 run.•She stormed the Capitol on Jan. 6. Then Gov. Ron DeSantis made her a state regulator. Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis signs legislation on Monday, May 15, 2023, banning state funding for diversity, equity, and inclusion programs at Florida's public universities, at New College of Florida in Sarasota, Fla. Douglas R. Clifford, Tampa Bay Times via AP More news to know now •What we know about a deadly Border Patrol shooting of an Arizona tribal member. •Could the 14th Amendment solve the debt limit standoff? The White House says it's not a fix.•Russia extended the detainment of American reporter Evan Gershkovich by three months. •The increasing imbalance of global wildlife signals a looming ''sixth extinction.''•On today's 5 Things podcast, Austin American-Statesman State Politics Reporter Niki Griswold reflects on the Uvalde shooting, one year later. Listen on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or your smart speaker. What's the weather today? Check your local forecast here. South Carolina Senate passes 6-week abortion ban A new abortion access battle is poised to begin all over again as a bill which bans abortions after six weeks of conception with exceptions for rape and incest is headed to South Carolina Gov. Henry McMaster's desk to become law. S.474 was passed Tuesday by the state's male dominated, Republican-controlled Senate. Five female senators — three Republicans, a Democrat and an Independent — continued efforts to filibuster the ban. Now abortion advocates have promised litigation as soon as the law takes effect. Read more •''A toxic issue'': How voters are forcing GOP contenders to talk about abortion ahead of 2024. •The Texas Senate advanced Medicaid expansion for new moms but added an abortion restriction.•HIV rates among young people are declining. But disparities persist. Florida school bans elementary students from reading Amanda Gorman's 'The Hill We Climb' poem Officials at a school in Miami-Dade County, Florida, on Tuesday banned elementary students from reading "The Hill We Climb," the poem Amanda Gorman famously recited at President Joe Biden's 2021 presidential inauguration. The poem, which has been published as a short book, will now be accessible only to middle school students at the Bob Graham Education Center in Miami Lakes. The former and first-ever National Youth Poet Laureate wrote on Instagram that she was "gutted" and said her publisher – Penguin Random House – is joining PEN America and others in a lawsuit to challenge book restrictions. Read more •The nation's largest LGBTQ advocacy group joined others in issuing a Florida travel advisory. •Florida restaurant Hamburger Mary's sues DeSantis for law restricting drag performances.•Target removed LGBTQ Pride month merchandise after threats. Youth Poet Laureate Amanda Gorman speaks during the inauguration of U.S. President Joe Biden on the West Front of the U.S. Capitol on January 20, 2021 in Washington, DC. Alex Wong, Getty Images Just for subscribers: •Mississippi River lock-and-dam system is outdated and in disrepair. What if it fails? •''Brighton ax murder'' case: James Krauseneck appeals conviction from beyond the grave. •Kratom supplier owes millions to family of South Florida nurse who overdosed and died. •''The show must go on'': Students raise $80K to perform canceled play with LGBTQ+ characters. Brazilians protest outside Spanish consulate after Vinicius Júnior's racism row About 100 Brazilian protesters gathered outside the Spanish consulate in São Paulo on Tuesday to condemn the racist abuse of Real Madrid forward Vinícius Júnior in Spain’s top-flight soccer league. The demonstrations come as three people have been detained in Spain in connection with racist taunts aimed at the Brazilian soccer star. The incident occurred Sunday at Valencia, when Vinícius, playing for Real Madrid, was met with racist jeers before getting off the team bus and was the subject of racially tinged chants during the match. Read more •Barcelona loses to Valladolid, Raphinha shows message of support for Vinícius Júnior. A protester holds up a photo of Brazilian soccer star Vinicius Jr and a message that reads in Portuguese; "Fight racism" during a protest outside the Spanish Consulate in Sao Paulo, Brazil, Tuesday, May 23, 2023. Consider "box theory" before deciding.•Jayson Tatum scored 33 points, as the Celtics topped the Heat in Game 4.• Here are eight businesses run by AAPI women you should know about.•Netflix launched paid account sharing in the United States. Here's how much it will cost you. Photo of the day: Niall Horan claims 'The Voice' victory with Gina Miles win The final showdown for the Season 23 title was between Team Blake singer Grace West and Team Niall singer Gina Miles Tuesday night. West performed a soaring cover of Shelton’s “Lonely Tonight” on the finale stage, and she was joined by her loyal coach for the performance. Miles and Horan slowed things down with a soulful rendition of “New York State of Mind” by Billy Joel. It turns out Horan made the right choice in the end, as Miles was declared the winner of Season 23, giving the freshman coach his first victory on the show. Subject: Her for deg i påsken!
Body: Legevakten er åpen hele påsken Legevakten på Volvat Majorstuen og i Bergen Laguneparken er åpen 365 dager i året. Vi holder åpent til kl.22 i Oslo og til kl.16 i Bergen hele påsken. Mer om legevakt Tilgjengelig på chat, video og telefon Hvis du trenger legehjelp så er vi tilgjengelige hele påsken på chat, video og telefon. Få legehjelp hjemmefra eller ute på tur - rett på din mobil. Få hjelp der du er Ikke glem solkrem i påskesola Etter en lang vinter er huden ekstra sårbar for den skarpe påskesola. Visste du at snøen kan forsterke solstrålene med opptil 50%? Enten du skal til fjells eller nyte fridagene i byen, er solbeskyttelse like viktig. Mer om solbeskyttelse Trenger du hjelp med allergi? Nå er pollensesongen fullt i gang! Våre erfarne leger hjelper både barn og voksne med råd, utredning og lindring av dine allergiplager. Velkommen til oss! Mer om allergi Volvat er Norges største tilbyder av private helsetjenester. © Volvat Medisinske Senter AS Facebook Instagram Ønsker du ikke å høre fra oss? Meld deg av nyhetsbrev

Her for deg i påsken!

Legevakten er åpen hele påsken Legevakten på Volvat Majorstuen og i Bergen Laguneparken er åpen 365 dager i året. Vi holder åpent til kl.22 i Oslo og til kl.16 i Bergen hele påsken. Mer om legevakt Tilgjengelig på chat, video og telefon Hvis du trenger legehjelp så er vi tilgjengelige hele påsken på chat, video og telefon. Få legehjelp hjemmefra eller ute på tur - rett på din mobil. Få hjelp der du er Ikke glem solkrem i påskesola Etter en lang vinter er huden ekstra sårbar for den skarpe påskesola. Visste du at snøen kan forsterke solstrålene med opptil 50%? Enten du skal til fjells eller nyte fridagene i byen, er solbeskyttelse like viktig. Mer om solbeskyttelse Trenger du hjelp med allergi?
